feynman.0: Put a charged particle in an electric field and it feels a push or a pull — no contact, nothing touching it — in exactly the same way a mass feels a tug in a gravitational field. On this page we'll work out the radial field that spreads out from a single point charge, the nice uniform field you get between a pair of parallel plates, and the energy a field hands over to the charges it shoves around.
feynman.1: Every charge stuffs the space around it with an electric field. Here's what the field strength E at a distance r from a point charge Q really means: it's the force the field would lay on each coulomb of charge you drop in there, and we measure it in newtons per coulomb (N C⁻¹).
feynman.2: To pin down which way the field points, you imagine a tiny positive test charge and ask which way it gets pushed: the field at any point is in the direction of the force on that small positive charge sitting there. So the field of a positive charge points straight outward in every direction, and the field of a negative charge points straight inward — the very same picture as the gravitational field around a point mass, as shown in Figure 3.1. Gravity only ever has the inward version, because mass comes in just one flavour and only ever attracts. When you use the formula, put in the magnitude of Q and then tack on the direction afterward from the sign of the charge.
feynman.3: Since E goes as 1/r², here's the consequence: double the distance and the field drops to a quarter, triple it and you're down to a ninth. Let's try a real one. At r = 0.30 m from a point charge Q = +2.0 μC: E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹, pointing radially away from the charge.
feynman.4: Two point charges push or pull on each other along the line joining their centres. The size of each force comes from Coulomb's law:
feynman.5: Like charges shove each other apart and unlike charges pull together. And those two forces are a Newton's third law pair: the force that q2 puts on q1 is equal in size and opposite in direction to the force that q1 puts on q2 — and that stays true even when one charge dwarfs the other.
feynman.6: Key idea The electric field at a point is the force per coulomb that the field would put on a positive test charge sitting there. A negative charge gets pushed the opposite way to the field.
feynman.7: Take two parallel metal plates, hook them up to a power supply so one's positive and one's negative, and you get a uniform electric field in the gap between them: same size, same direction, at every single point. The field lines march straight across from the positive plate to the negative plate, parallel and evenly spaced, as shown in Figure 3.2. Real plates fray a little — the field curves and bulges near the edges — but all through this course, just take the field between the plates as uniform.
feynman.8: The units V m⁻¹ and N C⁻¹ are exactly the same thing; use whichever you like for electric field strength. The field puts a force on any charge you set down in the gap:
feynman.9: Because the field is uniform, the force is the same no matter where the charge sits in the gap, so the charge accelerates at a steady rate — the electric twin of a mass falling in the uniform gravitational field near the Earth's surface. The field pushes a positive charge along the field direction, from the positive plate toward the negative plate; it pushes a negative charge the opposite way.
feynman.10: For plates 25 mm apart with 5.0 kV across them: E = V/d = (5.0 × 10³) ÷ (2.5 × 10⁻²) = 2.0 × 10⁵ V m⁻¹. The force the field puts on an electron in the gap is F = qE = (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) × (2.0 × 10⁵) = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ N, pointing toward the positive plate.
feynman.11: A charge crossing the gap feels a steady force the whole way across, so the field does work on it. When a charge q moves through a potential difference V, the work the field does is
feynman.12: This is just the Unit 1 definition of potential difference read backwards: one volt is one joule per coulomb, so a charge of q coulombs crossing V volts picks up qV joules. Now look hard at what's not in that equation: the plate separation d.
feynman.13: Trap "Wider plates give the charge more room to speed up, so it ends up with more kinetic energy." Nope — that's wrong, and here's why. Hold the voltage fixed and double d: you halve the field (E = V/d) and so you halve the force (F = qE); the charge travels twice as far but under half the force, so the work W = Fd comes out exactly the same. The kinetic energy a charge gains crossing the gap depends only on q and V — never on d.
feynman.14: If the charge starts from rest and the electric force is the only thing acting, then all that work turns into kinetic energy. This combined form is the one you'll find printed on the VCAA formula sheet:
feynman.15: The energies of single particles come out as awkwardly tiny numbers in joules, so in this area of study we lean on the electron-volt instead: 1 eV is the energy one electron picks up moving through a potential difference of one volt, so 1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. You can run the conversion either way. An electron accelerated through 2.5 kV gains 2.5 × 10³ eV = (2.5 × 10³) × (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J; and going the other direction, an energy of 8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J is (8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 5.0 × 10³ eV. Here's a handy thing: for any particle carrying one elementary charge, the energy in eV is just the accelerating voltage in volts — same number.
feynman.16: Connects In Unit 4 we'll turn W = qV into a measuring tool: in the photoelectric effect, the stopping voltage that just brings the fastest photoelectrons to a halt reads their kinetic energy straight off in electron-volts.
feynman.17: Every particle accelerator there is, the Australian Synchrotron included, starts with an electron gun. A heated filament — the cathode — lets electrons loose; a potential difference V0 between that cathode and an anode flings them across an evacuated gap; and the beam shoots out through a little hole in the anode, as shown in Figure 3.3.
feynman.18: To find the speed they come out at, just use ½mv² = qV. For V0 = 2.5 kV: v = √(2qV0/m) = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.5 × 10³) ÷ (9.11 × 10⁻³¹)) = 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, which is about 0.10c. You can forget about relativity here; in this area of study we take v to be way smaller than c.
feynman.19: Once it's moving, you can steer a charge around. In a uniform electric field, a charge that flies in at right angles to the field lines feels a steady force sideways to its original motion, so it curves along a parabola — exactly like a projectile arcing through a uniform gravitational field. In a uniform magnetic field it's different: the magnetic force always points square to the velocity, so the charge runs along a circular path at constant speed — its direction keeps changing but its kinetic energy never budges.
feynman.20: Splitting up the jobs this way is the accelerator model the study design asks for: uniform electric fields do the linear acceleration, because they do work and speed things up, while uniform magnetic fields do the turning, because they do no work at all. The two stages get bolted together into the full accelerator pipeline over on the motors and accelerators page.

sagan.0: A charged particle adrift in an electric field feels a force reach out and touch it across empty space, with nothing between them — exactly as a mass feels the pull of gravity. On this page we follow that influence in three of its guises: the radial field that streams outward from a single point charge, the uniform field penned between two parallel plates, and the energy a field pours into the charges it sweeps into motion.
sagan.1: Every charge fills the space around it with an electric field — an invisible presence radiating outward in all directions. The field strength E at a distance r from a point charge Q is simply the force the field would exert on each coulomb of charge set down at that place, measured in newtons per coulomb (N C⁻¹).
sagan.2: To fix the direction, we call on a single positive test charge: the field at any point lies in the direction of the force felt by a small positive charge placed there. So the field of a positive charge streams radially outward, away into space, while the field of a negative charge falls radially inward — the very same shape as the gravitational field surrounding a point mass, as shown in Figure 3.1. Gravity knows only the inward form, for mass carries but one sign and can only draw things together, never push them apart. In the formula, use the magnitude of Q alone, and let the sign of the charge tell you which way the field points.
sagan.3: Because E falls away as 1/r², doubling the distance dims the field to a quarter of its strength, and tripling it to a mere ninth — the field thins out swiftly as we retreat. Consider a worked case: at r = 0.30 m from a point charge Q = +2.0 μC, we find E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹, pointing radially away from the charge, out into the surrounding space.
sagan.4: Two point charges reach across the gap between them and exert forces upon each other, always along the straight line that joins their centres. The magnitude of each force is given by Coulomb's law:
sagan.5: Like charges push one another away; unlike charges draw together. And the two forces form a Newton's third law pair: the force exerted on q1 by q2 is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the force exerted on q2 by q1 — perfectly matched, even when one charge utterly dwarfs the other.
sagan.6: Key idea The electric field at a point is the force per coulomb that would be exerted on a positive test charge placed there. A negative charge feels a force turned exactly opposite to the field direction.
sagan.7: Set two parallel metal plates facing one another, wire them to a power supply so that one becomes positive and one negative, and the gap between them fills with a uniform electric field — a field of the same magnitude and the same direction at every single point. The field lines run straight across, from the positive plate to the negative, parallel and evenly spaced like the ranks of a marching column, as shown in Figure 3.2. Real plates let their fields curl and fringe near the edges; but throughout this course we shall take the field between the plates to be perfectly uniform.
sagan.8: The units V m⁻¹ and N C⁻¹ are one and the same; either may be used for the electric field strength. And the field lays its hand on any charge set down in the gap:
sagan.9: Because the field is uniform, the force is the same at every point in the gap, so the charge is swept along under constant acceleration — the electric twin of a mass falling freely in the uniform gravitational field near Earth's surface. The force exerted on a positive charge by the field runs in the field direction, from the positive plate toward the negative; the force exerted on a negative charge runs the opposite way.
sagan.10: For plates held 25 mm apart with 5.0 kV stretched across them, we find E = V/d = (5.0 × 10³) ÷ (2.5 × 10⁻²) = 2.0 × 10⁵ V m⁻¹. The force the field exerts on a single electron caught in the gap is F = qE = (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) × (2.0 × 10⁵) = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ N, directed toward the positive plate.
sagan.11: A charge that journeys across the gap feels a constant force along the whole of its path, so the field does work upon it — it gives the charge energy. When a charge q moves through a potential difference V, the work done by the field is
sagan.12: This is nothing but the Unit 1 definition of potential difference read in reverse: one volt is one joule for every coulomb, so a charge of q coulombs crossing V volts gathers up qV joules. And notice carefully what the equation does not contain: the plate separation d is nowhere to be found.
sagan.13: Trap “Wider plates give the charge more distance to accelerate, so surely more kinetic energy.” This is wrong, and instructively so. At a fixed voltage, doubling d halves the field (E = V/d) and so halves the force (F = qE); the charge travels twice as far but under half the force, so the work W = Fd comes out exactly unchanged. The kinetic energy gathered in crossing the gap depends only upon q and V — and never, ever upon d.
sagan.14: If the charge begins at rest and the electric force alone acts upon it, then every last scrap of the work emerges as kinetic energy. This combined form is the one printed on the VCAA formula sheet:
sagan.15: The energies of single particles are awkwardly tiny when measured in joules, so this area of study turns also to the electron-volt: 1 eV is the energy a single electron gathers in moving through a potential difference of one volt, so that 1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. The conversion runs both ways. An electron accelerated through 2.5 kV gains 2.5 × 10³ eV = (2.5 × 10³) × (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J; and going the other way, an energy of 8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J is (8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 5.0 × 10³ eV. For any particle that carries a single elementary charge, the energy gained in eV is numerically equal to the accelerating voltage in volts — a small and elegant gift of the units.
sagan.16: Connects Unit 4 will press W = qV into service as a measuring instrument: in the photoelectric effect, the stopping voltage that brings the swiftest photoelectrons to rest reads off their kinetic energy directly, in electron-volts.
sagan.17: Every particle accelerator ever built, the Australian Synchrotron among them, begins its work with an electron gun. A heated filament — the cathode — sets electrons free; a potential difference V0 between the cathode and an anode drives them across an evacuated gap; and the beam slips out through a small aperture in the anode, as shown in Figure 3.3.
sagan.18: The exit speed follows from ½mv² = qV. For V0 = 2.5 kV, we find v = √(2qV0/m) = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.5 × 10³) ÷ (9.11 × 10⁻³¹)) = 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — about 0.10c. You may set relativistic effects aside; this area of study assumes v remains far below c.
sagan.19: Once it is moving, a charge can be turned aside. In a uniform electric field, a charge entering at right angles to the field lines feels a constant force across its initial motion, and so traces out a parabola — the exact counterpart of a projectile arcing through the uniform gravitational field of Earth. In a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force stands forever perpendicular to the velocity, so the charge sweeps along a circular path at constant speed: its direction of travel changes ceaselessly, yet its kinetic energy never alters at all.
sagan.20: This dividing of labours is the accelerator model the study design asks of us: uniform electric fields supply the linear acceleration, for they do work and quicken the speed, while uniform magnetic fields supply the turning, for they do no work whatever. The two stages are assembled into the full accelerator pipeline on the motors and accelerators page.

bronowski.0: A charged particle set down in an electric field feels a force, and feels it across empty space, with nothing touching it — exactly as a mass feels the pull of gravity. This page follows that single idea through three of its forms: the field that spreads radially from a point charge, the uniform field standing in the gap between parallel plates, and the energy a field hands over to the charges it drives forward.
bronowski.1: Every charge, simply by existing, fills the space around it with an electric field. The strength of that field, E, at a distance r from a point charge Q, is nothing more than the force the field would press upon each coulomb of charge laid there — a quantity we measure in newtons per coulomb (N C⁻¹).
bronowski.2: To fix the direction we appeal to a positive test charge: the field at any point lies in the direction of the force a small positive charge would feel if placed there. So the field of a positive charge streams radially outward, and the field of a negative charge draws radially inward — the very shape we have already met in the gravitational field around a point mass, drawn for us in Figure 3.1. But gravity knows only the inward form, for mass carries a single sign and can do nothing but attract. Take the magnitude of Q in the formula, and let the sign of the charge tell you which way the field points.
bronowski.3: Because E falls away as 1/r², to double the distance is to cut the field to a quarter, and to triple it is to cut it to a ninth. Take a worked case: at r = 0.30 m from a point charge Q = +2.0 μC we have E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹, directed radially away from the charge.
bronowski.4: Two point charges reach out and press on one another along the line that joins their centres. The size of each force is what Coulomb's law tells us:
bronowski.5: Like charges drive each other apart; unlike charges draw together. bronowski.7: Take two parallel metal plates, join them to a power supply so that one is positive and one negative, and between them there appears a uniform electric field — a field with the same magnitude and the same direction at every point of the gap. The force a positive charge feels lies along the field, from the positive plate toward the negative; the force a negative charge feels points the other way.

greekepic.0: A charged thing set within an electric field is seized by a force that touches it not — even as a mass is seized within the field of gravity, hand laid upon it across the void. This song unfolds the radial field that streams from a single point of charge, the even field that dwells between two parallel plates, and the energy a field bestows upon the charges it drives onward.
greekepic.1: Every charge fills the space about it with an electric field, an unseen dominion. The field strength E at distance r from a point charge Q is the force the field would lay upon each coulomb of charge set in that place, reckoned in newtons for every coulomb (N C⁻¹).
greekepic.2: The direction is decreed by a positive test charge: the field at any point looks the way of the force borne upon a small positive charge set down there. Thus the field of a positive charge streams radially outward, and the field of a negative charge draws radially inward — the selfsame shape as the field of gravity about a point mass, as shown in Figure 3.1. The field of gravity wears only the inward form, for mass bears but one sign and draws all things to itself alone. Take the magnitude of Q in the formula, and from the sign of the charge bind the direction to it.
greekepic.3: Because E hangs upon 1/r², to double the distance is to cut the field strength to one quarter, and to treble it, to one ninth. Behold a worked case: at r = 0.30 m from a point charge Q = +2.0 μC: E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹, directed radially away from the charge.
greekepic.4: Two points of charge lay forces each upon the other, along the line that joins their centres. The magnitude of each force is proclaimed by Coulomb's law:
greekepic.5: Like charges drive one another apart, and unlike charges draw together. The two forces are a pair bound by Newton's third law: the force borne upon q1 by q2 is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the force borne upon q2 by q1, even when the one charge towers mighty over the other.
greekepic.6: Key idea The electric field at a point is the force for every coulomb that would be laid upon a positive test charge set in that place. A negative charge is seized by a force that runs counter to the field's direction.
greekepic.7: Two parallel plates of metal, joined to a power supply, the one positive and the one negative, raise an even electric field within the gap: the field keeps the same magnitude and the same direction at every point. The field lines run straight from the positive plate to the negative plate, parallel and evenly spaced, as shown in Figure 3.2. Real plates wear curved fringing fields near their edges; yet throughout this course, hold the field between the plates to be uniform.
greekepic.8: The units V m⁻¹ and N C⁻¹ are one and the same; either may serve for the electric field strength. The field lays a force upon any charge set within the gap:
greekepic.9: Because the field is even, the force is the same at every point within the gap, so the charge is borne onward at constant acceleration — the electric likeness of a mass within the even field of gravity near the surface of the Earth. The force laid upon a positive charge by the field looks the way of the field, from the positive plate toward the negative; the force laid upon a negative charge by the field looks the contrary way.
greekepic.10: For plates sundered by 25 mm with 5.0 kV set across them: E = V/d = (5.0 × 10³) ÷ (2.5 × 10⁻²) = 2.0 × 10⁵ V m⁻¹. The force laid upon an electron in the gap by the field is F = qE = (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) × (2.0 × 10⁵) = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ N, directed toward the positive plate.
greekepic.11: A charge that crosses the gap is held in the grip of a constant force across the whole distance, so the field does work upon it. When a charge q passes through a potential difference V, the work done by the field is
greekepic.12: This is the Unit 1 decree of potential difference read backward: one volt is one joule for every coulomb, so a charge of q coulombs crossing V volts wins qV joules. Mark well what the equation does not bear within it: the plate separation d.
greekepic.13: Trap "Wider plates grant the charge more distance over which to gather speed, and so more kinetic energy." This speaks falsely. At fixed voltage, to double d is to halve the field (E = V/d) and thus to halve the force (F = qE); the charge journeys twice the distance under half the force, so the work W = Fd stands unchanged. The kinetic energy won in crossing the gap hangs upon q and V alone — never upon d.
greekepic.14: If the charge sets forth from rest and the electric force alone lays hand upon it, all of the work rises up as kinetic energy. This joined form is the one graven upon the VCAA formula sheet:
greekepic.15: The energies of single particles are inconveniently small when reckoned in joules, so this area of study calls also upon the electron-volt: 1 eV is the energy won by one electron passing through a potential difference of one volt, so 1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. The conversion runs both ways: an electron driven through 2.5 kV wins 2.5 × 10³ eV = (2.5 × 10³) × (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J; and contrariwise, an energy of 8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J is (8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 5.0 × 10³ eV. For any particle bearing one elementary charge, the energy won in eV stands numerically equal to the accelerating voltage in volts.
greekepic.16: Connects Unit 4 will wield W = qV as a measuring instrument: in the photoelectric effect, the stopping voltage that brings the swiftest photoelectrons to rest reads off their kinetic energy straightway in electron-volts.
greekepic.17: Every particle accelerator, the Australian Synchrotron among them, begins with an electron gun. A heated filament, the cathode, looses electrons; a potential difference V0 between the cathode and an anode drives them across an evacuated gap; the beam comes forth through a small aperture in the anode, as shown in Figure 3.3.
greekepic.18: The exit speed follows from ½mv² = qV. For V0 = 2.5 kV: v = √(2qV0/m) = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.5 × 10³) ÷ (9.11 × 10⁻³¹)) = 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, about 0.10c. You may pass over relativistic effects; this area of study holds v to be far less than c.
greekepic.19: Once set in motion, a charge may be turned aside. Within an even electric field, a charge entering at right angles to the field lines is seized by a constant force perpendicular to its first motion, so it follows a parabolic path — the exact likeness of a projectile within the even field of gravity. Within an even magnetic field, the magnetic force lies forever perpendicular to the velocity, so the charge follows a circular path at constant speed: the direction of motion is altered, yet the kinetic energy is not.
greekepic.20: This sundering of offices is the accelerator model the study design demands: even electric fields grant linear acceleration, for they do work and quicken the speed, while even magnetic fields grant the change of direction, for they do no work at all. The two stages are joined into the full accelerator pipeline on the motors and accelerators page.

cnpop.0: 带电粒子往电场里一站，啥都没碰到，就被一股力推着走——这操作跟质量待在引力场里被拽着的剧情一模一样。这一页咱们就把三件事捋明白：点电荷周围那个朝四面八方辐射的场、两块平行板之间规规矩矩的匀强场，还有电场是怎么把能量喂给它加速的那些电荷的。
cnpop.1: 每个电荷都不安分，往自己周围的空间里铺满了电场。在离点电荷 Q 距离为 r 的地方，场强 E 说白了就是：你往那儿扔一库仑的电荷，场会给它来多大的力，单位是牛每库仑(N C⁻¹)。
cnpop.2: 方向这事儿，全听一个正的试探电荷的：某点的场指向哪，就是放一个小正电荷在那点时受力指向哪。所以正电荷的场是从中心往外辐射、四散奔逃，负电荷的场是从外朝里、统统收拢——这跟点质量周围的引力场长得是一个模子，看图 3.1 就懂。引力场可没这么多花样，它只有往里收的那一种，因为质量只有一个符号，只管吸、从不推。套公式时 Q 取大小就行，方向另外按电荷的正负贴上去。
cnpop.3: 因为 E 是吃 1/r² 这碗饭的，距离翻一倍，场强直接掉到四分之一；距离翻三倍，那就只剩九分之一了，跌得相当干脆。来个实战例子：在离点电荷 Q = +2.0 μC 距离 r = 0.30 m 的地方，E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹，方向沿着半径往外、背离电荷。
cnpop.4: 两个点电荷会沿着连心线互相较劲。每个力的大小，库仑定律说了算：
cnpop.5: 同号互推，异号相吸，老规矩。而这两个力是一对正经的牛顿第三定律搭档：q2 施加在 q1 上的力，跟 q1 施加在 q2 上的力，大小一样、方向相反——哪怕其中一个电荷比另一个大出一大截，这俩也是平起平坐，谁也别想占便宜。
cnpop.6: Key idea 某点的电场，就是放一个正试探电荷在那儿时、它每库仑会挨到的力。要是放的是负电荷，那它受的力方向跟场正好反着来。
cnpop.7: 接上电源的两块平行金属板，一块正一块负，就在缝隙里造出了一个匀强电场：场在每一点的大小和方向都长一个样，整整齐齐。场线从正板笔直冲向负板，根根平行、间距还均匀，看图 3.2。当然真实的板子边缘会有弯弯绕绕的边缘场；但在这门课里，咱们一律当板间是匀强场来处理。
cnpop.8: V m⁻¹ 和 N C⁻¹ 这俩单位其实是同一个东西换了身衣服，电场强度用哪个都行。只要往缝隙里搁个电荷，场就给它来一股力：
cnpop.9: 因为场是匀强的，缝隙里每一点的力都一个样，所以电荷吃到的是恒定加速度——这正是匀强引力场里地表附近那个质量的电学翻版。正电荷受的力跟场同向，从正板奔向负板；负电荷受的力则反着来，往相反方向走。
cnpop.10: 两板间距 25 mm、两端压上 5.0 kV：E = V/d = (5.0 × 10³) ÷ (2.5 × 10⁻²) = 2.0 × 10⁵ V m⁻¹。缝隙里一个电子被场施加的力是 F = qE = (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) × (2.0 × 10⁵) = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ N，方向指向正板。
cnpop.11: 一个电荷横穿这条缝隙，全程都被恒力推着，所以场对它做了功。当电荷 q 穿过一段电势差 V 时，场做的功是
cnpop.12: 这其实就是 Unit 1 里电势差定义反着读一遍：一伏特就是一焦每库仑，所以 q 库仑的电荷跨过 V 伏特，就能进账 qV 焦耳。但请你瞪大眼睛盯着这个式子里没出现的那个家伙：板间距 d。
cnpop.13: Trap 有人会脑补："板子拉得越宽，电荷加速的路程越长，那不就攒到更多动能了嘛。"打住，这是错的。电压定死的情况下，d 翻一倍，场就被腰斩(E = V/d)，力也跟着腰斩(F = qE)；电荷走了两倍的路、却只挨到一半的力，所以功 W = Fd 纹丝不动。横穿缝隙攒到的动能只认 q 和 V 这两个——跟 d 半毛钱关系都没有。
cnpop.14: 要是电荷从静止出发、又只有电场力在干活，那这功就原封不动全变成了动能。这个合体形态，正是印在 VCAA 公式表上的那个：
cnpop.15: 单个粒子的能量拿焦耳来量，小得让人尴尬，所以这块知识点还祭出了电子伏特这把尺子：1 eV 就是一个电子穿过一伏特电势差攒下的能量，于是 1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J。换算两头都能跑：一个电子被 2.5 kV 加速，攒到 2.5 × 10³ eV = (2.5 × 10³) × (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J；反过来，8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J 这能量就是 (8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 5.0 × 10³ eV。对任何只带一个基本电荷的粒子来说，用 eV 表示的能量，数值上跟加速电压的伏特数一模一样，连换算都省了。
cnpop.16: Connects 到了 Unit 4，W = qV 摇身一变成了量测仪器：在光电效应里，那个刚好把跑得最快的光电子刹停的截止电压，直接就把它们的动能用电子伏特给读了出来。
cnpop.17: 每一台粒子加速器，澳大利亚同步加速器也不例外，都是从一把电子枪开始发车的。一根烧得通红的灯丝(就是阴极)放出电子；阴极和阳极之间一段电势差 V0 把它们越过真空缝隙猛地加速一通；电子束最后从阳极上一个小孔里冲出去，看图 3.3。
cnpop.18: 出口速度从 ½mv² = qV 里就能抠出来。取 V0 = 2.5 kV：v = √(2qV0/m) = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.5 × 10³) ÷ (9.11 × 10⁻³¹)) = 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹，差不多 0.10c。相对论效应你大可不必操心；这块知识点默认 v 比 c 小得多。
cnpop.19: 一旦动起来，电荷就能被掰弯方向。在匀强电场里，一个垂直于场线杀进来的电荷，会挨上一股垂直于它初始运动方向的恒力，于是走出一条抛物线——这跟匀强引力场里的抛体根本就是同一出戏。而在匀强磁场里，磁力永远跟速度垂直，所以电荷以恒定速率走圆形路径：运动方向一直在变，动能却纹丝不动。
cnpop.20: 这种分工正是学习设计点名要的加速器模型：匀强电场负责直线加速，因为它们做功、把速度往上顶；匀强磁场负责拐弯转向，因为它们压根不做功。这两道工序最后会在电动机与加速器那一页里，被拼装成完整的加速器流水线。

ai.0: Drop a charged particle into an electric field and it feels a push or a pull — no contact required — in exactly the way a mass feels its weight in a gravitational field. On this page you’ll explore the radial field that fans out from a point charge, the tidy uniform field trapped between parallel plates, and the energy a field hands over to the charges it sends flying.
ai.1: Every charge quietly drapes the space around it in an electric field. The field strength E at distance r from a point charge Q tells you the force the field would press on each coulomb of charge you parked there — measured in newtons per coulomb (N C⁻¹).
ai.2: Which way does the field point? Let a tiny positive test charge decide: the field at any spot points along the force that would shove a small positive charge sitting there. So the field of a positive charge bristles radially outward, while the field of a negative charge rushes radially inward — the very same shape as the gravitational field hugging a point mass, as shown in Figure 3.1. Gravity only ever has the inward version, because mass comes in one flavour and only ever attracts. In the formula, feed in the magnitude of Q and pin the direction on afterwards from the sign of the charge.
ai.3: Because E hangs on 1/r², stepping twice as far away knocks the field down to a quarter, and three times as far down to a ninth. Let’s run a case: at r = 0.30 m from a point charge Q = +2.0 μC, E = kQ/r² = (8.99 × 10⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁻⁶) ÷ (0.30)² = 2.0 × 10⁵ N C⁻¹, pointing radially away from the charge.
ai.4: Two point charges tug or shove on each other straight along the line joining their centres. How big is each force? Coulomb’s law has the answer:
ai.5: Like charges drive each other apart and unlike charges pull together. And here’s the elegant part: the two forces are a Newton’s third law pair — the force q2 exerts on q1 matches the force q1 exerts on q2, equal in size and opposite in direction, even when one charge utterly dwarfs the other.
ai.6: Key idea The electric field at a point is the force per coulomb the field would exert on a positive test charge sitting there. A negative charge feels a force pointing the opposite way to the field.
ai.7: Wire two parallel metal plates to a power supply — one positive, one negative — and the gap between them fills with a beautifully uniform electric field: same magnitude, same direction, at every single point. The field lines march straight from the positive plate to the negative plate, parallel and evenly spaced, as shown in Figure 3.2. Real plates fray a little at the edges with curved fringing fields, but throughout this course just take the field between the plates as uniform.
ai.8: The units V m⁻¹ and N C⁻¹ are one and the same — reach for whichever you like when quoting electric field strength. And that field pushes on any charge dropped into the gap:
ai.9: Since the field is uniform, the force is identical everywhere in the gap, so the charge accelerates at a constant rate — the electric twin of a mass falling in the uniform gravitational field near Earth’s surface. The field pushes a positive charge along the field direction, from the positive plate toward the negative one; a negative charge gets pushed the opposite way.
ai.10: Take plates 25 mm apart with 5.0 kV across them: E = V/d = (5.0 × 10³) ÷ (2.5 × 10⁻²) = 2.0 × 10⁵ V m⁻¹. The force the field presses on an electron in the gap is F = qE = (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) × (2.0 × 10⁵) = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ N, aimed toward the positive plate.
ai.11: A charge crossing the gap feels that constant force the whole way across, so the field does work on it. When a charge q slides through a potential difference V, the work the field does is
ai.12: This is just the Unit 1 definition of potential difference read backwards: one volt is one joule per coulomb, so a charge of q coulombs sweeping through V volts pockets qV joules. Notice carefully what’s missing from the equation — the plate separation d.
ai.13: Trap “Wider plates give the charge more room to accelerate, so it ends up with more kinetic energy.” Tempting, but wrong. At fixed voltage, doubling d halves the field (E = V/d) and so halves the force (F = qE); the charge travels twice as far under half the force, so the work W = Fd doesn’t budge. The kinetic energy gained crossing the gap rides on q and V alone — never on d.
ai.14: If the charge sets off from rest and the electric force is the only one acting, every bit of that work turns up as kinetic energy. This combined form is the one you’ll find printed on the VCAA formula sheet:
ai.15: Energies of single particles come out laughably tiny in joules, so this area of study leans on the electron-volt instead: 1 eV is the energy one electron picks up crossing a potential difference of one volt, so 1 eV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. The conversion runs both ways — an electron accelerated through 2.5 kV gains 2.5 × 10³ eV = (2.5 × 10³) × (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J; and going the other direction, an energy of 8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶ J is (8.0 × 10⁻¹⁶) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 5.0 × 10³ eV. For any particle lugging a single elementary charge, the energy gained in eV is simply the accelerating voltage in volts — same number, no arithmetic needed.
ai.16: Connects Unit 4 will press W = qV into service as a measuring tool: in the photoelectric effect, the stopping voltage that just halts the fastest photoelectrons reads their kinetic energy straight off in electron-volts.
ai.17: Every particle accelerator on Earth — the Australian Synchrotron included — kicks off with an electron gun. A heated filament, the cathode, sheds electrons; a potential difference V0 between cathode and anode hurls them across an evacuated gap; and the beam slips out through a small aperture in the anode, as shown in Figure 3.3.
ai.18: How fast does it come out? Work it from ½mv² = qV. For V0 = 2.5 kV: v = √(2qV0/m) = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.5 × 10³) ÷ (9.11 × 10⁻³¹)) = 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹, roughly 0.10c. You can safely ignore relativistic effects here; this area of study assumes v stays well below c.
ai.19: Once it’s moving, a charge can be steered. Send it into a uniform electric field at right angles to the field lines and it feels a constant force square to its original motion, so it arcs along a parabola — the spitting image of a projectile in a uniform gravitational field. Send it into a uniform magnetic field instead and the magnetic force always sits perpendicular to the velocity, so the charge sweeps a circular path at constant speed: the direction keeps changing but the kinetic energy never does.
ai.20: This neat division of labour is exactly the accelerator model the study design asks for: uniform electric fields handle the linear acceleration, because they do work and crank up the speed, while uniform magnetic fields handle the turning, because they do no work at all. The two stages bolt together into the full accelerator pipeline on the motors and accelerators page.
